topic: A person finds out their surprise party has been cancelled
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the soft, sickening thud of a dropped weight. My eyes scanned the subject line—"Party Update"—and then they snagged on the body text. A cold, hollow sensation bloomed in my chest, quickly spreading outward. I reread the sentence three times. Canceled. The silence in my apartment, usually a comforting blanket, suddenly felt deafening, pressing in on my eardrums. My fingers hovered over the keyboard, useless, as if my hands had forgotten their purpose. I slowly lowered the phone, the screen reflecting a vacant look in my own eyes. A strange lightness washed over me, followed immediately by a heavy, sinking feeling in my stomach. I could only stare at the blank wall opposite me, processing the sheer absurdity of it all.

[story 2]

I was halfway through making celebratory cocktails, the music already queued up, when the notification popped up. My hand froze mid-air, a glass of gin tilting precariously. I blinked, forcing myself to look again. The message confirmed it: the gathering, the one I’d meticulously planned, the one everyone had been anticipating, was off. A strange fizzing sensation started in my throat, a sudden dryness that made swallowing difficult. I dropped the shaker onto the counter with a clatter that echoed too loudly in the quiet room. I walked to the window, staring out at the streetlights, feeling a profound disconnect from the reality around me. My jaw felt suddenly slack, and I could feel a warmth creeping up my neck, a confusing mix of deflation and utter bewilderment.

[story 3]

When Sarah forwarded the memo, I didn't move from the sofa. The crisp white paper seemed to warp in my vision. Every word swam before my eyes, refusing to settle into coherent meaning. I traced the cancellation notice with a fingertip, the texture of the paper oddly abrasive against my skin. A low, involuntary sound escaped my lips—a soft, breathy noise that held no real inflection. My shoulders slumped forward, a heavy surrender to the unexpected news. I picked up the remote control, intending to change the channel, but my hand trembled so violently that the device slipped from my grasp, landing silently on the rug. The world seemed to have tilted on its axis; everything felt suddenly too bright, too loud, and utterly meaningless.
